coredata.dc:title: Visualizing BPA by molecularly imprinted ratiometric fluorescence sensor based on dual emission nanoparticles
coredata.dc:creator: Lu, Hongzhi
Xu, Shoufang
coredata.dc:description: Construction of ratiometric fluorescent probe often involved in tedious multistep preparation or complicated coupling or chemical modification process. The emergence of dual emission fluorescent nanoparticles would simplify the construction process and avoids the tedious chemical coupling. Herein, we reported a facile strategy to prepare ratiometric fluorescence molecularly imprinted sensor based on dual emission nanoparticles (d-NPs) which comprised of carbon dots and gold nanoclusters for detection of Bisphenol A (BPA). D-NPs emission at 460nm and 580nm were first prepared by seed growth co-microwave method using gold nanoparticles as seeds and glucose as precursor for carbon dots. When they were applied to propose ratiometric fluorescence molecularly imprinted sensor, the preparation process was simplified, and the sensitivity of sensor was improved with detection limit of 29nM, and visualizing BPA was feasible based on the distinguish fluorescence color change. The feasibility of the developed method in real samples was successfully evaluated through the analysis of BPA in water samples with satisfactory recoveries of 95.9–98.9% and recoveries ranging from 92.6% to 98.6% in canned food samples. When detection BPA in positive feeding bottles, the results agree well with those obtained by accredited method. The developed method proposed in this work to prepare ratiometric fluorescence molecularly imprinted sensor based on dual emission nanoparticles proved to be a convenient, reliable and practical way to prepared high sensitive and selective fluorescence sensors.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Dual emission nanoparticle
Molecularly imprinted polymers
Ratiometric fluorescent probe
BPA

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast doctopic grantnumber grantsponsor highlightsabst primabst ref

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis mesoporous structured BPA imprinted ratiometric fluorescence sensor
Selectivity and sensing specificity of the sensor
Practical application to real sample analysis

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The process of prepared d-NPs by seed growth co-micorwave methed. B The effect of glucose dosage on the FL spectrum of d-NPs: a. 0mg glucose; b. 0.2mg glucose c. 0.5mg glucose, and d. 1mg glucose was added into 10mL gold seeds. C were the TEM images of AuNCs and d-NPs.

(A) Schematic illustration of the fluorescence response of the d-NPs to BPA. And (B) was the preparation process for M-R-MIPs@d-NPs by one step concentration of TEOS. And C was the TEM and SEM images of M-R-MIPs@d-NPs and M-R-NIPs@d-NPs.

The corresponding fluorescence spectra of the ratiometric probes exposure to different concentrations of BPA. A was the M-R-MIPs@d-NPs, B was M-R-NIPs@d-NPs, C was the M-MIPs@AuNCs, and D was the selective experiment (inset was the competitive experiment) of M-R-MIPs@d-NPs and M-R-NIPs@d-NPs. The experimental conditions were probes, 40mg/L; excited light, 380nm; silt widths of excitation and emission, 5 and 5nm, respectively. The inset photos was the fluorescence colors of probes with addition of different concentration of BPA under 365nm UV light, and the concentration unit of BPA was μM. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
Spiked recoveries and relative standard deviations for detection of BPA in spiked samples by M-R-MIPs@d-NPs and compared with HPLC.
Result±RSD% of the analysis of positive feeding bottle samples using M-R-MIPs@d-NPs and National standard method.

head.ce:title: Visualizing BPA by molecularly imprinted ratiometric fluorescence sensor based on dual emission nanoparticles

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Linyi University, Linyi 276005, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Linyi University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Construction of ratiometric fluorescent probe often involved in tedious multistep preparation or complicated coupling or chemical modification process. The emergence of dual emission fluorescent nanoparticles would simplify the construction process and avoids the tedious chemical coupling. Herein, we reported a facile strategy to prepare ratiometric fluorescence molecularly imprinted sensor based on dual emission nanoparticles (d-NPs) which comprised of carbon dots and gold nanoclusters for detection of Bisphenol A (BPA). D-NPs emission at 460nm and 580nm were first prepared by seed growth co-microwave method using gold nanoparticles as seeds and glucose as precursor for carbon dots. When they were applied to propose ratiometric fluorescence molecularly imprinted sensor, the preparation process was simplified, and the sensitivity of sensor was improved with detection limit of 29nM, and visualizing BPA was feasible based on the distinguish fluorescence color change. The feasibility of the developed method in real samples was successfully evaluated through the analysis of BPA in water samples with satisfactory recoveries of 95.9–98.9% and recoveries ranging from 92.6% to 98.6% in canned food samples. When detection BPA in positive feeding bottles, the results agree well with those obtained by accredited method. The developed method proposed in this work to prepare ratiometric fluorescence molecularly imprinted sensor based on dual emission nanoparticles proved to be a convenient, reliable and practical way to prepared high sensitive and selective fluorescence sensors.

ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescence sensors displayed obvious advantage in trace substance detection because of their high sensitivity, simplicity and cost-effective instrumentation. In recent years, many fluorescent nanoparticles, such as quantum dots (QDs) (Peng et al., 2016；Pu et al., 2016), carbon dots (CDs) (Wang et al., 2016a; Sun et al., 2016) and noble metal nanoclusters (Gasper and Ramasubramaniam, 2016; Douglas-Gallardo et al., 2016; Goswami et al., 2016； Chang and Ho, 2015), have been employed to achieve high sensitive detection and imaging. Among those novel fluorescent nanoparticles, gold nanoclusters (AuNCs) displayed many distinguished advantages in sensing and labeling owing to their good stability, excellent fluorescent property, low cytotoxicity and biocompatibility (Goswami et al., 2016；Chang and Ho, 2015). AuNCs can be synthesized by chemical reduction of gold salt in the presence of stabilizing agents. For example, bovine serum albumin or thiolate protected AuNCs for detection of reactive oxygen species (Chen et al., 2013), copper ions (Han et al., 2015), mercury ions (Chang et al., 2014), tyrosinase activity (Teng et al., 2015) or dopamine (Tao et al., 2013) have been reported.

Compared to single emission fluorescent sensors, the ratiometric fluorescent probe has widely attracted attention due to its sensitivity and the built-in correction for avoiding environmental effects. Accompanied with fluorescent dyes or other fluorescent nanoparticles, AuNCs have been employed for construction of ratiometric fluorescent probe as signal response group or reference (Wang et al., 2016a, 2014; Qiao et al., 2015). It is well-known that an attractive ratiometric fluorescent probe should consist of three parts: a signal response or signal output section, a proportional part, and a linker. So construction of ratiometric fluorescent probe often involved in tedious multistep preparation and sophisticated coupling or chemical modification process. The emergence of dual emission fluorescent nanoparticles (d-NPs) would simple the construction process and avoids the tedious chemical coupling (Shangguan et al., 2016; Zhao et al., 2015; Zhang et al., 2016; Qu et al., 2012). For example, Wang reported a novel dual emission carbon dots synthesized by simple one-pot hydrothermal treatment of citric acid and basic fuchsin for ratiometric intracellular pH sensing (Shangguan et al., 2016). Nie developed a co-template method to synthesize a quantum dot-AuNCs dual-emission fluorescent nanohybrid to achieve ratiometric fluorescent biosensing and bioimaging in vitro and in vivo (Zhao et al., 2015). Huang et al. synthesized gold-carbon dots d-NPs by microwave assisted method for ratiometric fluorescence cellular imaging (Zhang et al., 2016). The emergence of d-NPs would simplify the construction process of AuNCs based ratiometric fluorescent sensors.

In the meanwhile, as a versatile functional polymer, molecularly imprinted polymers (MIPs) have flourished in recent decades and displayed promising application potentials in chemo/biosensors because of its high selectivity, high stability and ease of preparation (Chen et al., 2011, 2016). Combined the high selectivity of MIPs with the high sensitivity of fluorescence sensor, fluorescence MIPs sensors displayed many distinguish advantages when detection of trace substances in complicated matrix. Furthermore, combined with ratiometric fluorescence technique, which can provide visual signal output observed by the naked eyes, MIPs ratiometric fluorescence probes for TNT (Xu and Lu, 2015a), melamine (Xu and Lu, 2015b), 2,4-D (Wang et al., 2016b) and phycocyanin (Wang et al., 2016d) have confirmed the advantage of ratiometric fluorescence technique in improving sensitivity and visual signal output. In those previous work, MIPs ratiometric fluorescence detection was achieved by two fluorescence nanoparticles (or dyes) based on core-shell structure or one-pot synthesis method. For example, in our previous work, red CdTe QDs and green CdTe QDs were employed for ratiometric fluorescence detection of TNT based on core-shell structure (Xu and Lu, 2015b). CdTe QDs as signal output and hematoporphyrin as reference were simultaneously adopted for detection of melamine by one-pot synthesis method (Xu and Lu, 2015b). Commonly, core-shell structured ratiometric fluorescence sensor often involved in time consuming and complicated two steps, while the one-pot synthesis method often involved in complicated condition optimization.

Inspired by the above mentioned studies, we aspired to synthesis d-NPs and then to construct a MIPs ratiometric fluorescence sensor based on d-NPs, which comprise of AuNCs and CDs, to achieve simple, fast and selective visualizing detection. As one important phenolic environmental estrogens, bisphenol A (BPA) can cause estrogenic endocrine disruption and promoting tumor progression (Wu et al., 2015). Considering the adverse properties of BPA and the fact that BPA can quench AuNCs (Wu et al., 2015), BPA was used as the target analyst to confirm the proof-of-concept. In the meanwhile, considering that mesoporous silica displayed obvious advantages in improving sensitivity of MIPs fluorescence sensor (Xu and Lu, 2015b), mesoporous silica was employed as imprinting matrix.

In this work, we report a facile strategy to prepare mesoporous structured molecularly imprinted ratiometric fluorescence sensor based on d-NPs for high sensitive and selective visualizing detection of BPA. First, d-NPs were synthesized through seed growth co-microwave method in domestic microwave oven using gold nanoparticles as seeds and glucose as the precursor of CDs. Then mesoporous structured MIPs ratiometric fluorescence sensor was prepared using d-NPs as both signal response and proportional group, BPA as template, APTES as functional monomer. By one step condensation of tetraethylorthosilicate (TEOS) in the presence of cetyltrimethylammoniumbromide (CTAB), d-NPs and BPA, mesoporous structured MIPs ratiometric fluorescence sensor was prepared. The study provided a new technology for construction of MIPs ratiometric fluorescence sensors and promoted the research progress of the synthesis and application of dual emission nanoparticles.

Glucose, reduced glutathione (GSH), BPA, estradiol, phenol, cholesterol and mercury chloride were purchased from Tianjin Reagent Plant (Tianjin, China). Hydrogen tetrachloroaurate trihydrate (HAuCl4·3H2O), tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS), cetyltrimethylammonium bromide (CTAB) and 3-aminopropyl triethoxy silane (APTES) were purchased from J&K Technology Ltd. Ultrapure water (18.2MΩ, Millpore Co., USA) was used in all experiments. All chemicals used were of analytical grade without any further purification.

A transmission electron microscope (TEM, JEM-2100) and a scanning electron microscope (SEM, Hitachi S-4800 FE−SEM) were used for morphological evaluation. A Fluoromax-4 Spectrofluorometer (Horiba Scientific) was used for fluorescence measurements. A UV-3600 ultraviolet spectrophotometry (Shimadzu, Japan) was employed for UV–Vis spectra recording. An AGL-9406 portable UV lamp with 365nm and 254nm emission was used for taking photos. The types of element in d-NPs were evaluated by energy dispersive spectrometer analysis (SEM-EDS, Japan). The surface diffractogram of the d-NPs was characterized by X-ray diffraction on an X′Pert PRO X-ray diffractometer with a Cu Ka radiation source (XRD, PANalytical, Netherlands).

D-NPs were prepared by seed growth method in microwave oven as reported with minor modification (Zhang et al., 2016). First, gold seeds were prepared as follows: 5mL of 20mM freshly prepared HAuCl4 aqueous solution and 1.5mL of 100mM GSH were added into 44.5mL ultrapure water. The mixture was heated to 70°C for 2h under general stirring to form gold seeds. Then d-NPs were prepared by microwave method. 0.2mg glucose was dissolved into 10mL freshly prepared gold seeds, then the solution was microwaved in a domestic microwave for 10min. The obtained product was dissolved again and centrifuged (12,000rpm, 10min) to remove agglomerated particles. At last, the bright yellow d-NPs were obtained.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis mesoporous structured BPA imprinted ratiometric fluorescence sensor
ce:section.ce:para: Mesoporous structured BPA imprinted ratiometric fluorescence sensors (M-R-MIPs@d-NPs) were prepared by one-pot sol-gel synthesis method. Briefly, 0.7mL NaOH (2M) was added to 100mL of CTAB solution (5.5×10−3 M). The mixture was heated to 70°C, followed by the addition of 1.0mL of TEOS, 200μL of APTES，45.6mg BPA and 5mL d-NPs. After reaction at 70°C for 2h, the precipitate was collected and washed repeatedly with a mixture of water/ethanol under ultrasound assistance to remove template BPA and CTAB. The control non-imprinted ratiometric fluorescence sensor (M-R-NIPs@d-NPs) was prepared as described above but without template BPA. The control single emission fluorescence sensor (M-MIPs@AuNCs) was prepared using single emission AuNCs institute for d-NPs.

The binding capacity of as prepared M-R-MIPs@d-NPs and M-R-NIPs@d-NPs was evaluated by adsorption experiment. Typically, BPA was added into 5.0mL 100mg/L M-R-MIPs@d-NPs or M-R-NIPs@d-NPs solution with final concentration of 10μM and 20μM, and placed on the oscillator at room temperature for 2h. Then the supernatant were separated by centrifugation and detected by HPLC. The selective binding capacity was evaluated using estradiol, phenol and cholesterol as control with the same process.

The BPA detection procedure was carried out in aqueous solution. In a typical run, BPA was added into 4.0mL of 40μg/mL probe solution one by one with the final concentrations of 100nM to 5.0μM. After incubation 10min, the fluorescence spectra were collected under excitation at 380nm with slit widths of 5/5nm.

In order to test the selectivity of M-R-MIPs@d-NPs, estradiol, phenol, cholesterol and mercury ions were examined by the similar procedure mentioned above while the concentration of template and controls were fixed as 1μM. During competitive experiment, increase the ratio of phenol to BPA from 0.5 to 5.0 by changing the concentration of phenol while BPA was fixed at 1μM.

Water samples were used to demonstrate the applicability of the M-R-MIPs@d-NPs sensor for BPA detection in aqueous matrices. Collected Yi River water and tap water samples were filtered through 0.45µm pore size membrane to remove the suspended particles, and then concentrated with BPA at 0.5, 1 and 2μM respectively. Then M-R-MIPs@d-NPs was added with final concentration of 40mg/L followed by fluorescence detection. Canned milk powder and canned fish purchased from local supermarket were chosen as the model analytes and the analyses were performed as reference (Grumetto et al., 2008). Briefly, 50g of canned food added with BPA was dissolved in 10mL of acetonitrile by ultrasonic extraction 10min and then centrifuged. The precipitate was extracted with a fresh 10mL of acetonitrile again. The two acetonitrile supernatant were put together and partitioned with hexane (10mL╳3 times) by shaking vigorously for 1min for fat removal. The acetonitrile extract was dried by nitrogen and dissolved in10.0mL of a water/acetonitrile 9:1 (v/v) mixture and detected by M-R-MIPs@d-NPs. Blank samples were prepared as the same procedure without being spiked. Feeding bottles which were provided by Food Quality Supervision Bureau of Linyi was cut into pieces, and then boiled in water for 6h. Then the water was filtered and cool to room temperature and volume for further detection. As control, the BPA in feeding bottles also were detected by National Standard Method. Briefly, feeding bottles were cut into pieces and soaked in boiling water for 6h in glass bottle. Before HPLC detection using C18 column, SPE pretreatment using ProElut PLS GLASS cartridge was necessary.

D-NPs were the key point for the M-R-MIPs@d-NPs. So d-NPs were first synthesized and characterized. Inspired by the previous work (Zhang et al., 2016), d-NPs were prepared by seed growth co-microwave method, and the process was displayed in Fig. 1 A. Firstly, gold seeds were synthesized by reduction of HAuCl4 by reduced GSH at 70℃ for 2h. Then d-NPs with two emission at 460nm and 580nm were prepared by co-microwave gold seed and glucose in domestic microwave oven for 10min. Previous reported method for thiolate or BSA protected AuNCs often involved in relatively long reaction time (1day) and elevated temperatures. CDs by hydrothermal method often need higher temperature (200℃ for 8h). Using the co-microwave method, the preparation time was sharply shorted, and the process was simplified.

Further experiment was carried out to confirm the two emissions. From Fig. 1 B we can see that without glucose, only 10mL gold seeds were microwaved, only single emission at 580nm was observed. When glucose was added into the gold seeds, another emission at 460nm was appeared. With the increasing of glucose, the emission at 460nm increased while the emission at 580nm decreased. The reason for the increasing glucose resulted in the decreasing at 580nm would be the follow. During the process of preparation of d-NPs, carbon will first nucleate on the surface of gold seeds, then to form CDs. The growth rate of CDs was much higher than AuNCs, and the formation of CDs would influence the growth of gold cluster. From XRD pattern we can see that two broad peaks ascribe to highly disordered carbon atoms can be observed, while the characteristic peaks of elemental Au were weak. We hypothesized that with the increasing of the amount of glucose, more and more surface of gold seed were occupied, and the growth of gold nanoclusters was affected, so the fluorescence intensity of the gold clusters was weakened. At the same time, with the increasing of the amount of glucose, more and more CDs are generated, so the fluorescence intensity of the CDs increased. For the d-NPs, emission at 460nm ascribed to CDs while 580nm was the character emission of AuNCs. It should be pointed out that the fluorescence intensity of CDs was much higher than those of AuNCs. Very less mount of glucose would resulted in distinguish change of the fluorescence spectrum of d-NPs from dual emission to single emission. When 1.0mg glucose was employed, only one emission at 460nm can be observed.

To demonstrate that the dual emission arises from a single type of particles rather than simple mixture of CDs with AuNCs, the control experiment was carried out. From Fig. S1 we can see that when 10mL gold seeds was microwave for 10min, bright yellow solvent with single emission at 580nm displayed orange fluorescence was obtained. When 0.5mg of glucose was dissolved into 10mL ultrapure water and followed by microwave for 10min, no fluorescence emission can be observed and the solvent was colorless, which indicated that no CDs were formed. However, when 0.5mg of glucose was added to 10mL gold seeds, dual emission at 460nm and 580nm with blue fluorescence can be observed. More importantly, the fluorescence intensity at 460nm was much higher than that at 580nm. The possible mechanism has been discussed (Zhang et al., 2016). The formation of the nanoparticles involved in nucleation and growth. CDs has a low nucleation rate but a high growth rate, while gold has a low growth rate but a high nucleation rate. Therefore, when the carbon precursors are mixed with the gold seeds, carbon will preferentially nucleate on the surface of negative potential gold seeds to form d-NPs. It should be pointed out clearly that microwave high concentration of glucose (such as 1g/10mL) can produce CDs, maybe because the high concentration is favor for nucleation. The TEM image of AuNCs and d-NPs were displayed in Fig. 1C. As can be seen, d-NPs showed to be monodispersed with average size of about 3nm, which is similar to that AuNCs. The FT-IR spectra (Fig. S2) show the surface functional groups of the synthesized AuNCs and d-NPs, which displayed that AuNCs and d-NPs enjoy the same functional groups. There main peaks located at 3452cm−1 (νO−H), 1657cm−1 (νC˭O) and 1382 (ν−C−N) were observed. There was a shoulder peak at 405nm in the UV–vis spectra for AuNCs. For the d-NPs, an obvious blue shift can be observed, and the shoulder peak shifted to 360nm (Fig. S2). XRD pattern of d-NPs display two broad peaks centered at 2θ=20° and 32°, which ascribe to highly disordered carbon atoms, and four weak peaks centered at 2θ=37°, 52°, 63° and 76°, which were the characteristic peaks of elemental Au. For the EDS analysis, the weight percent of Au element was 65.49 for AuNCs while the value was 45.87 for d-NPs. The weight percent of C element increased to 27.62 for the d-NPs, demonstrated the successful preparation of d-NPs.

To demonstrate the utility and feasibility of the as-prepared d-NPs for visualizing detection. BPA can quench AuNCs at 580nm (Wu et al., 2015) and tartrazine can quench CDs (Xu and Lu, 2015b) at 460nm were evaluated. From Fig. S3 we can see that fluorescence emission at 460nm quenched by tartrazine also resulted in the decreasing of fluorescence intensity at 580nm, while fluorescence emission at 580nm quenched by BPA resulted in the increasing of fluorescence intensity at 460nm. Under 365nm UV light, fluorescence color changed from orange to green, denoting that the d-NPs could serve as a highly efficient fluorescent probe to detect BPA. The UV adsorption spectrum (Fig. S4) indicated that the existence of FRET between CDs and AuNCs, while CDs was the donor and AuNCs as acceptor. Considering from the sensitivity and visualizing detection feasibility, fluorescence emission of AuNCs at 580nm was selected as the response signal and CDs was chosen as proportional groups.

Reference reported that the fluorescence quenching of the AuNCs may result from the energy transfer or photo-induced electron transfer (Tao et al., 2013). The mechanism of energy transfer could be excluded because there was no overlap between the absorption spectra of BPA and the emission spectra of AuNCs. Meisenheimer complex between BPA and the primary amino groups on the surface of the AuNCs could be formed which resulted from the electron transfer. Then the photo-induced energy of AuNCs would be transferred to the complex, leading to fluorescence quenching of AuNCs (Wu et al., 2015).

In our previous work, we have summarized that when compared to fluorescence nanoparticles sensors, the sensitivity of MIPs fluorescence sensors often were weakened. The phenomena maybe attributed to the high-crosslinked nature of MIPs. Mass transfer was hindered in sphere or irregular bulk MIPs particles, and the direct and efficient interaction between target molecules and fluorescence nanoparticles would be hindered, which resulted in the weak response signal. To reduce mass transfer resistance was an effective method to improve detection sensitivity. So mesoporous silica was adopted as imprinting matrix. M-R-MIPs@d-NPs was prepared by a simple one-step sol-gel condensation process, which were showed in Fig. 2. BPA imprinted M-R-MIPs@d-NPs was prepared by condensation TEOS in the presence of APTES-BPA-APTES complex and d-NPs. CTAB was used as soft template to form mesoporous structure. After removal of CTAB and BPA, M-R-MIPs@d-NPs for recognition of BPA were obtained. The whole preparation process was simple, time-saving and easy of control.

The morphology of M-R-MIPs@d-NPs and corresponding M-R-NIPs@d-NPs were characterized by TEM and SEM. From Fig. 2 C we can see that template BPA did not displayed obvious effect on the particles size and morphology. For both MIPs and NIPs, the average diameter was about 130nm and the mesoporous structure can be observed clearly. Binding property of M-R-MIPs@d-NPs were investigated using M-R-NIPs@d-NPs as control. The static and selective binding capacities were displayed in Fig. S5. The adsorption capacity of M-R-MIPs@d-NPs increased from 13.6 to 27.4mg/g when initial BPA concentration increased from 10μM to 20μM. On the same BPA concentration, the binding capacity of M-R-MIPs@d-NPs was much higher than M-R-NIPs@d-NPs. Imprinting factor a, defined as Q MIP/Q NIP was calculated to be 4.8 and 6.1, respectively. For the selective binding experiment, MIPs displayed higher binding capacity for BPA, while the values for phenol, estradiol and cholesterol were lower. In the meanwhile, the binding capacity of M-R-NIPs@d-NPs for the four compounds were similar and much lower than the values of MIPs. The high imprinting factors implied the high selectivity of MIPs, which ascribed to the special cavities of BPA formed during the imprinting process.

The amount of probe has a great impact on the detection sensitivity, so the amount of probe was optimized. From Fig. S6 we can see that the fluorescent intensity both at 580nm and 460nm increased with increasing of the amount of M-R-MIPs@d-NPs from 20mg/L to 50mg/L. The addition of BPA resulted in the decrease of I580 and increase of I460. After calculated the slope of the quenching (Table S1), we can see that when the amount of M-R-MIPs@d-NPs fixed at 40mg/L, the probe displayed the highest sensitivity. From Fig. S7 we can see that 5min was enough for the probe to reach equilibrium. The fast response rate can be attributed to the mesoporous structure. So in the following work, the amount of M-R-MIPs@d-NPs fixed at 40mg/L and the response time was fixed as 5min. After the variables were optimized, the dose response of M-R-MIPs@d-NPs to BPA were examined. In the absence of BPA, the ratiometric probe displayed two well-resolved emission peaks at 460 and 580nm. Upon the addition of BPA, the fluorescence intensity at 580nm was continuously quenched while the intensity at 460nm increased, as shown in Fig. 3. The ratio of the fluorescence intensity I460/I580 was closely related to the amount of BPA, and exhibited an excellent linear relationship to the concentration of BPA ranging from 100nM to 2.5μM, which was used for quantification of BPA with the detection limit as low as 29nM (3S/N), which is much lower than the permitted maximum contaminant level of 0.2μM in environmental water safety monitoring. It presented a high sensitivity compared with other reported detection method for BPA (Wu et al., 2015). The high sensitivity comes from two aspects. First, mesoporous structure which was favorable for mass transfer was employed for imprinting matrix. Second, ratiometric fluorescence technique displayed higher sensitivity when compared to single fluorescence detection. A slight decrease of the intensity at 580nm could result in distinguish increase at 460nm, and then resulted in obvious fluorescence colors changes continuously from orang to green to blue, as demonstrated in the inset of Fig. 3A. Therefore, the visual detection of BPA by naked eyes is feasible.

The dose response of the M-R-NIPs@d-NPs to BPA also was examined. From Fig. 3 B we can see that fluorescence intensity at 580nm was slightly quenched by BPA with lower sensitivity and narrower linear range, meanwhile the fluorescence color change was not obviously. Combined the static binding experiment results, this phenomenon can be explained as no specific recognition sites existed in the NIPs, so BPA did not have any chance to enter into the inner of the NIPs. Fluorescence intensity of d-NPs can be quenched by those non-specific binding BPA located on the surface of materials. The ratio of slopes of the standard linear, K SV, MIP to K SV, NIP, was defined as imprinting factor (IF) to evaluate the selectivity of the materials. Under optimum conditions, IF was calculated to be 4.65, indicating the better selectivity of M-R-MIPs@d-NPs.

Meanwhile, the advantages of the ratiometric fluorescence probe was verified by comparison with single fluorescence probes, in which single emission AuNCs were embedded. As can be seen from Fig. 3, the color changes of the single fluorescence probe upon the addition of BPA were hard to observe. The comparison clearly showed the higher sensitivity and reliability of ratiometric fluorescence probe for visual detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity and sensing specificity of the sensor
ce:section.ce:para: For an excellent MIPs chemosensor, high selectivity is a matter of necessity. The selectivity of M-R-MIPs@d-NPs was demonstrated using template analogues (phenol, estradiol and cholesterol) and metal ion (Hg2+) as control. The ratios of fluorescence intensity (I460/I580) was recorded. About 40% of fluorescence intensity at 580nm was quenched by 1.0μM BPA, accompanied with remarkable fluorescence color changes under UV light from orange to green. In contrast, no obvious changes in fluorescence intensity ratio and color can be seen after the addition of control compounds. The results implied the high selectivity of M-R-MIPs@d-NPs for BPA. It is well known that AuNCs can be quenched by Hg2+ remarkably (Chang et al., 2014). However, in BPA imprinted M-R-MIPs@d-NPs, no selective recognition sites for metal ions were existed, so the quenching of metal ions can be prevented.

Furthermore, competitive experiments were performed by varying the ratio of phenol to BPA while fixed concentration of BPA at 1.0μM. As shown in Fig. 3, when the concentration of BPA was fixed, the quenching amount displayed no significant change with the increase of the C phenol/C BPA. Both the selectivity and competitive experiments confirmed the fact that M-R-MIPs@d-NPs had specific recognition ability toward BPA.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Practical application to real sample analysis
ce:section.ce:para: Though the concentration of BPA in water is very low because of its poor solubility, it can be biologically enriched and amplified. So phenols, which come from plastics, dyes, pesticides, paper, etc, are important organic pollutants in water and the harm of BPA in water is not negligible. Development of novel sensor with high sensitivity for BPA in water samples was necessary. So in this work, the practical application of M-R-MIPs@d-NPs was investigated by detection of BPA in water samples. Spiking experiment was carried out because BPA did not be detected in tap water and river water samples. The estimated recoveries and RSD were shown in Table 1 and the results were compared with HPLC. The results indicated that the recoveries of BPA for real water samples were ranging from 95.9% to 100.8% with RSD lower than 4.0%. More importantly, the detection results were much similar to those mature HPLC method.

BPA-containing products are widely used in foodstuffs packaging. Because of its high fat soluble, BPA can migrate from food contact material to foodstuffs. So foods and beverages can be contaminated with BPA. Then the canned milk powder and canned fish were chose as real samples to verify the practicability of the method. The recoveries experiments were carried out by the standard addition method in order to evaluate the accuracy of the developed method. The recoveries at three spiked levels ranging from 92.6% to 98.6% with the RSD in the range of 2.7–3.7% verified the good accuracy and reliability of the method.

The method was further applied to test positive feeding-bottle samples that were contaminated with BPA ranging from 0.5 to 2.0μM. The samples were analyzed using M-R-MIPs@d-NPs and the results were compared with National Standard Method ( Table 2). From Table 2 we can see that the values in our method agree well with those obtained by accredited method. The results demonstrated that M-R-MIPs@d-NPs was an efficient and effective methodology for sensitive assessment of BPA at low concentration.

In summary, d-NPs with dual emission at 460nm and 580nm were prepared by seed growth co-microwave method, and then applied to propose mesoporous structured ratiometric fluorescence probe for high sensitive and selective detection of BPA. The convenient co-microwave strategy avoids the tedious chemical coupling and reduced synthetic time for propose of ratiometric fluorescence probe. The as-prepared M-R-MIPs based on d-NPs proved to be simple, rapid, and reliable in detection of BPA in real samples. We expected that the d-NPs could pave the way for developing novel ratiometric fluorescence probe for future high sensitive detection.
